coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive detection of methamphetamine in environmental water bodies by an Au@carbon dots/chitosan nanocomposite modified electrochemical aptasensor
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Chunying
Shu, Mushui
Yang, Caini
Ding, Ding
Xu, Zhizhen
Wang, Jingyi
Wang, Dongsheng
coredata.dc:description:
Background The robust analytical methods for methamphetamine (METH), the second most widely used illicit drug globally, in aqueous environments are essential for on-site detection of regional drug abuse and pollution, and then timely control its threat to ecological environment and public health.
Results A simple and label-free electrochemical aptasensor based on aptamer/Au@carbon dots/chitosan nanocomposite modified gold electrode (Apt/Au@CDs/CS/AuE) was firstly developed for measuring trace METH in environmental water bodies. The aptamer was immobilized on Au nanoparticles through Au–N bond, where carbon dots provided large surface area for good dispersion of nanomaterials. Under optimal conditions, the aptasensor could detect METH from 10−3–103 ng L−1 with a detection limit of 0.87 pg L−1 and present satisfactory reproducibility, stability and specificity. The developed aptasensor was applicable to common pH and wide temperature and ionic strength range, free from the interference of organic matters, such as humic acid, polysaccharides-like and protein-like substances, whereas suspended particle was a significant influencing factor during METH detection. The aptasensor showed feasibility for METH detection in four kinds of real waters (drinking water, river water, lake water and untreated wastewater) with recoveries of 92.4–104.6 %.
Significance The developed aptasensor provides a promising sensing platform for quick testing of illicit drug in aqueous environments. The water quality indicators are crucial influencing elements to be considered in the design and application of the electrochemical biosensors for trace pollutants detection in environmental water bodies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical biosensor
Illegal drug
Methamphetamine
Aptasensor
Water quality
Nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication process of the electrochemical aptasensor based on Au@CDs/CS nanocomposites for METH detection.
(a–b) TEM and HRTEM images of Au@CDs, (c) UV–vis spectra of Au@CDs, (d) XPS survey spectrum of Au@CDs, and high resolution XPS spectra of (e) C 1s, and (f) Au 4f.
(a) CV, (b) DPV and (c) EIS recorded on bare AuE, Au@CDs/CS/AuE, Apt/Au@CDs/CS/AuE, BSA/Apt/Au@CDs/CS/AuE and METH/BSA/Apt/Au@CDs/CS/AuE.

(a) SWV response of the aptasensor with varied METH concentrations (0, 0.001, 0.005, 0.1, 1, 10, 100, and 1000 ng L−1). (b) Calibration curve showing the plot of ΔJ vs logarithm of METH concentration. (c) Reproducibility of the five parallel aptasensors for detecting 0.1 ng L−1 METH. (d) Stability of the aptasensors during 15 days for 0.1 ng L−1 METH determination. (e) Specificity of the aptasensors towards 100 mg L−1 HA, SA and BSA and 0.1 ng L−1 METH. (f) Selectivity of the aptasensors towards METH (0.1, 1 and 10 ng L−1) in the presence of the interfering substances HA, SA, and BSA with concentration of 100 mg L−1.

Specificity of the aptasensors towards 1 μg L−1 AMP, MDMA, MDA, 6-AM, EPH and PSE; METH concentration 0.1 ng L−1; Mix: the mixture of 0.1 ng L−1 METH, 1 μg L−1 six analogues and 100 mg L−1 HA, SA and BSA.
Impacts of different water quality indicators on electrochemical signals of the aptasensor for the detection of 0.1, 1 and 10 ng L−1 METH, (a) pH, (b) temperature, (c) SPM, and (d) IS.
The CD spectrum of Apt and Apt-METH complex at pH 5 and 8 and IS 5 and 100 mM. The concentration of Apt was 10 μM.
The atomic force microscope and SEM images of the electrodes before and after SPM (400 and 4000 mg L−1) incubation.
Measurement of the METH concentrations by the developed electrochemical aptasensors in the four spiked water samples (tap water, river water, lake water and wastewater).

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive detection of methamphetamine in environmental water bodies by an Au@carbon dots/chitosan nanocomposite modified electrochemical aptasensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu, Sichuan, 610500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu, Sichuan, 610500, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Urban Safety and Environmental Science, Beijing Academy of Science and Technology, Beijing, 100054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Urban Safety and Environmental Science
Beijing Academy of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Urban Safety and Environmental Science, Beijing Academy of Science and Technology, Beijing, 100054, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental and Resource Sciences, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, 310058, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental and Resource Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental and Resource Sciences, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, 310058, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Co-first author: Chunying Yuan and Mushui Shu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The robust analytical methods for methamphetamine (METH), the second most widely used illicit drug globally, in aqueous environments are essential for on-site detection of regional drug abuse and pollution, and then timely control its threat to ecological environment and public health.

A simple and label-free electrochemical aptasensor based on aptamer/Au@carbon dots/chitosan nanocomposite modified gold electrode (Apt/Au@CDs/CS/AuE) was firstly developed for measuring trace METH in environmental water bodies. The aptamer was immobilized on Au nanoparticles through Au–N bond, where carbon dots provided large surface area for good dispersion of nanomaterials. Under optimal conditions, the aptasensor could detect METH from 10−3–103 ng L−1 with a detection limit of 0.87 pg L−1 and present satisfactory reproducibility, stability and specificity. The developed aptasensor was applicable to common pH and wide temperature and ionic strength range, free from the interference of organic matters, such as humic acid, polysaccharides-like and protein-like substances, whereas suspended particle was a significant influencing factor during METH detection. The aptasensor showed feasibility for METH detection in four kinds of real waters (drinking water, river water, lake water and untreated wastewater) with recoveries of 92.4–104.6 %.

The developed aptasensor provides a promising sensing platform for quick testing of illicit drug in aqueous environments. The water quality indicators are crucial influencing elements to be considered in the design and application of the electrochemical biosensors for trace pollutants detection in environmental water bodies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Worldwide drug abuse as a public health issue is of increasing concern. Methamphetamine (METH) is known as the second most widely used illicit drug in the world after marijuana. The addiction of METH, an addictive central nervous system stimulant, can pose significant health risk and serious threat to society [1]. It is imperative to develop reliable, portable and highly sensitive methods for METH field screening to control the consequences of its abuse. Given that most of illicit drugs (about 62 % of METH) are excreted to sewage from urine and faeces within 24 h after an oral dose [2], wastewater-based epidemiology (WBE) has been applied to monitor the consumption of illicit drugs in different communities by measuring the concentration of illicit drugs in wastewater and further tracing the level of illicit drug consumption in the area [3,4]. Furthermore, the illicit drugs unremoved by sewage treatment plant may be discharged into urban rivers and lakes, even drinking water source, endangering the ecological environment and human health [5–7]. To ensure the accuracy and timeliness for the monitoring data of the trace illicit drugs (ng L−1) in aqueous environments, the fast and sensitive methods are essential [8–10].

At present, the common method for detecting illicit drugs in aqueous environments is the liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [11]. There have been other methods reported for METH detection in waters, such as gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [12,13] and optical sensors [14–16], etc. However, these methods are usually characterized by expensive instruments, high specialization or complicated sample pretreatment, which do not satisfy the real-time monitoring of illicit drugs in waters. The electrochemical aptasensor, combing the electrochemical analysis technique and the specific recognition of aptamer (Apt), is an emerging tool for monitoring trace targets, with the features of rapid response, low cost, high specificity and user-friendly control, etc. [17]. More importantly, no complicated enrichment for samples is necessary due to the excellent sensitivity of the electrochemical aptasensor combining with nanotechnology [18–21].

Electrochemical aptasensors have been developed for the detection of biomarkers in biological fluids, such as serum, urine, and saliva at ultra-low level. While the detection of illicit drugs in water samples by electrochemical aptasensor was rarely reported. In 2016, Yang's team [22] firstly developed the aptasensor by immobilizing the Apt on a gold chip for electrochemical detection of cocaine in wastewater. Samples remain to be preconcentrated before measurement, resulting from the unfavorable sensitivity of the developed aptasensor. During the fabrication of electrochemical biosensor, the electrodes modified by different nanomaterials are an effective strategy to obtain improved sensitivity [23,24]. Particularly, the synthesis of nanocomposites containing metal and carbon-based nanomaterials is an applicable approach, which can overcome the inherent defects of single nanomaterial, such as liable to aggregation or poor conductivity [25–27].

In addition, natural waters and wastewaters as complex matrices contain various inherent substances, such as the dissolved organic matter (DOM), suspended particulate matters (SPM), soluble ions, etc. These substances combing with the environmental variables (e.g., pH and temperature) may influence the stability of illicit drugs in water samples, thereby increasing the uncertainties of qualitive and quantitative detections [28]. Thus, these water quality indicators should be considered when developing electrochemical aptasensors for accurately detecting trace targets in water samples. However, to date, the relationship between the water quality indicators and the electrochemical responses of aptasensors has received little attention.

In this work, an ultra-sensitive and selective electrochemical aptasensor based on Au@carbon dots/CS (Au@CDs/CS) nanocomposites were designed for trace METH detection in water samples. As a few microliters of water sample containing METH was incubated on the Apt/Au@CDs/CS modified electrode surface, the METH/Apt complexes formed, and electrochemical reaction of the redox probe was inhibited resulting in the change of electrochemical signal, which was monitored as the output of METH detection (Fig. 1 ). Through the optimization of the fabrication conditions, the aptasensor demonstrates satisfactory reproducibility, stability and sensitivity. For the first time, the effects of water quality indicators including pH, temperature, suspended particles, ionic strength (IS) and DOM on bio-electrochemical detection of METH were investigated. The developed aptasensor was applicable for METH detection in various real water samples (drinking water, river, lake and wastewater). This work is expected to provide a simple and feasible strategy for quick testing of trace illicit drugs using a tiny amount of water samples, avoiding tedious steps of extraction and concentration.

The sequence of the METH Apt used in this study was 5′–NH2–(CH2)6-ACGGTTGCAAGTGGGGACTCTGGGTAGGCTGGGGTAATTTGG-3' (Shanghai Bioengineering Co., Ltd.) [14,29]. All the chemicals, materials and equipment used in this study are described in detail in the Supplementary Material (Section S1.1-1.3).

Firstly, the gold electrode (AuE) was cleaned by sonicating with ethanol and ultrapure water, respectively, followed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) treatment in 0.5 mol L−1 H2SO4 (potential: 0.2 to 1.6 V, sweep rate 0.5 V s−1) to remove oxides and impurities from the AuE surface until reproducible CV curves were observed [30]. Subsequently, 10 μL of Au@CDs/CS nanocomposite (preparation procedures shown in Section S1.4) was dropped on the cleaned AuE and dried on a hot plate. The electrode was then immersed in 0.25 % glutaraldehyde (Gla) solution for 1 h. Gla was covalently linked to the surface of Au@CDs/CS through the –CHO groups binding with the –NH2 groups on chitosan [31]. Afterwards, the electrode was incubated with 10 μL of Apt (1 μM) for 8 h. The amino-modified Apt was immobilized on the electrode surface through the Au–N bond (formed by Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) and-NH2 groups on Apt) and CN bond (formed by the condensation reaction of –NH2 groups on Apt with the –CHO groups on Gla). After incubation, the electrode was rinsed with deionized water and immersed in PBS buffer solution for 2 h to remove excess Apt. Finally, 10 μL BSA solution (1 %) was dropped onto the surface of modified electrode for 1 h to inhibit the non-specific binding [31]. Fig. 1 presents the preparation process of Apt/Au@CDs/CS/AuE aptasensor. The electrochemical analysis of METH by the fabricated aptasensor is shown in Section S1.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of water quality indicators on detection
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the effect of DOM on the electrochemical response and the sensitivity and selectivity of the constructed aptasensors, humic acid (HA), sodium alginate (SA) and bovine serum protein (BSA), which represent three typical DOMs, i.e., humus-like, polysaccharides (extracellular polymeric substances)-like and protein-like substances, were used. The solutions of HA, SA and BSA with concentration of 100 mg L−1 were incubated on the aptasensors, respectively. The current responses on the DPV curves before and after incubation were recorded and compared with that of 0.1 ng L−1 METH. Besides, 100 mg L−1 HA, SA and BSA with three concentrations of METH (0.1, 1 and 10 ng L−1) were also tested.

The effects of other water quality indicators, pH (5–8), temperature (4–30 °C), SPM (0–400 mg L−1), and IS (0–100 mM) on the METH detection were analyzed by recording the electrochemical response on the aptasensor in 0.1, 1 and 10 ng L−1 METH with varied water compositions. The kaolin suspensions with concentration of 30–400 mg L−1 were prepared to model the SPM in aqueous environment. The IS of water samples was adjusted to 0–100 mM by mixing the Na2SO4, Na2CO3 and KCl solutions (1:1:1) with different concentrations, which contained the common coexisting salt ions in waters.

The real water samples of drinking water, river water, lake water and untreated wastewater were collected from the tap water in the laboratory, the river at the Southwest Petroleum University, the Gui-lake and the inlet of a sewage plant in Chengdu, respectively. After collection, the water samples were stored in the dark at 4 °C before use. The water quality indicators of the water samples, i.e., pH, dissolved organic carbon (DOC), turbidity, conductivity and dissolved ions, were measured. The water samples were directly used without further treatment.

The morphology of CDs and Au@CDs characterized by TEM and HRTEM in Fig. 2 a–b proved the successful preparation of Au@CDs nanocomposite with the CDs homogeneously distributing around the AuNPs, since the lattice spacing of 0.237 nm and 0.217 nm corresponded to the (111) plane of Au and the [10, 0] planar spacing of graphitic carbon, respectively (detailed analysis seen in Section S2 and Fig. S2). The formation of AuNPs was further confirmed by the change of color and UV–vis spectrum of the CDs solution before and after reaction with HAuCl4. The CDs solution was light yellow in natural light and emitted a clear blue fluorescence under UV light irradiation at 365 nm. After reacting with HAuCl4, the CDs solution color changed into purple due to the production of the AuNPs (the inset of Fig. 2c). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs showed two strong characteristic peaks at 243 nm and 315 nm attributed to the π-π∗ transition [32,33]. While the Au@CDs nanocomposite exhibited a pronounced absorption peak at 533 nm (Fig. 2c), belonging to the surface plasmon resonance absorption of AuNPs [34,35]. It suggested that the formation of AuNPs was through the reduction of HAuCl4 by CDs as a mild reducing agent. The FT-IR spectroscopic analysis of CDs into Au@CDs nanocomposite also indicated that CDs in-situ reduced HAuCl4 to AuNPs and combined with AuNPs, forming Au@CDs nanocomposite (Section S2).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed to further understand the composition and structure of the Au@CDs nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 2d, Au@CDs contained three typical characteristic peaks of Au, C, and O locating at 85.10 eV, 287.08 eV, and 532.53 eV, respectively. Fig. 2e–f showed the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s and Au 4f. The peaks at 284.80 eV, 286.51 eV, and 289.10 eV correspond to C–C, C–OH, and CO groups, respectively (Fig. 2e), suggesting that more oxygen-containing groups were presented in Au@CDs nanocomposite, which made the nanomaterials better dispersed in water [36]. Fig. 2f exhibited the Au 4f 7/2 and 4f 5/2 peaks at 83.97 eV and 87.65 eV, respectively, corresponding to Au0. This indicated the reduction of AuCl4 − to Au0 atoms. The difference of 3.68 eV between the two peaks also identified the presence of AuNPs [37,38]. In summary, the Au@CDs nanocomposite was successfully prepared through in-situ reduction by the CDs as the green reducing agent. The CDs were attached uniformly to the surface of AuNPs.

The electrochemical properties of the electrodes at different fabrication stages of the aptasensors were studied by CV, EIS and DPV techniques. As observed in Fig. 3 a, Au@CDs/CS/AuE (red curve) exhibited significantly higher redox peaks compared to the bare AuE (black curve). The current densities of anodic peak (J pa) and cathodic peak (J pc) were 17.79 μA mm−2 and 17.53 μA mm−2, respectively, and the separation of peak potentials (ΔE p = E pa-E pc) was 96 mV. According to the Randles-Sevcik equation [39], the electroactive areas of bare AuE and Au@CDs/CS/AuE are 0.30 cm2 and 0.39 cm2, respectively. Further Au@CDs/CS can effectively improve the electroactive area of the electrode. To verify the synergistic effect of AuNPs and CDs, the CV and DPV plots for bare AuE, CDs/CS/AuE, AuNPs/CS/AuE and Au@CDs/CS/AuE were also compared (Fig. S3). It can be seen that the Au@CDs/CS/AuE shows a stronger current response with a smaller peak separation (96 mV) relative to those of the bare AuE, AuNPs/CS/AuE and CDs/CS/AuE notably, indicating that the Au@CDs/CS nanocomposites greatly amplified the electrochemical signal of the aptasensor. It can be explained by the substantial increase of the specific surface area and electroactive sites of the modified electrode by the synergistic effect of AuNPs and CDs, promoting the rapid transfer of electrons on the electrode surface. When the Apt was immobilized on the surface of Au@CDs/CS/AuE, the current density was significantly reduced (J pa = 12.67 μA mm−2, J pc = 13.67 μA mm−2) and the ΔE p increased to 108 mV (blue curve). The immobilization of Apt enhanced the repulsive interaction of the negative charged [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− and phosphate groups in Apt, and thus hindered the electron transfer at the electrode surface. The current density further decreased with the introduction of BSA as a blocking agent (green curve), which could block the uncombined active sites and prevent non-specific adsorption on the electrode surface. After adding 0.1 ng L−1 METH to the electrode surface, a decrease in current signal was observed. Due to the specific binding of the target molecule METH with Apt through hydrogen bonding, van der Waals forces and electrostatic forces, the secondary structure of Apt underwent conformational changes, hindering the approach of redox probe molecules to the electrode surface for redox reactions. The DPV curves (Fig. 3b) showed the same variation pattern as the CV response.

The EIS spectra were recorded at an open circuit potential (OCP) of 0.276 V. The resulted Nyquist plots are presented in Fig. 3c. The impedance fitting was conducted using Zview2 software and the specific values are presented in Table S1. The obtained spectra were divided into two regions: a high-frequency semicircular portion corresponding to the electron transfer process and a low-frequency linear portion controlled by diffusion [40]. These spectra were fitted using the Randles equivalent circuit model in Fig. 3c. The maximum value of the semicircle corresponded to ωR ct C dl = 1 (ω: frequency; R ct: electron transfer resistance; C dl: the double-layer capacitance) [41]. The R ct value scan depended on the charge transfer rate, analyte concentration, and the electroactive surface area of the electrode; the larger the electroactive area, the smaller the interfacial resistance. The bare AuE exhibited a small semicircle with R ct density of 1.814 Ω mm−2. The resistance density of the Au@CDs/CS/AuE decreased to 0.822 Ω mm−2, due to the higher electrical conductivity of Au@CDs/CS nanocomposites, thus promoting the electron transfer and reducing the R ct at the electrode. When the Apt was incorporated on the electrode surface, the resistance density increased to 1.293 Ω mm−2. The anionic phosphate backbone of the Apt inhibited the charge migrating to the surface of the electrode and elevated the charge-transfer resistance. When METH bound to Apt, the semicircle seen in the EIS curve significantly increased (with a resistance density of 7.98 Ω mm−2), indicating METH could hinder the charge transfer and enhance the resistance of electrode. All of the above results proved that the Au@CDs/CS nanocomposites could substantially amplify the electrochemical signal of the aptasensor and the electrochemical aptasensor was constructed as expected.

To optimize the aptasensor performance, the optimal raw materials ratio of Au@CDs (1:4), incubation conditions of Apt on the modified electrode (0.8 μM Apt with 8 h at 25 °C) and the hybridization time of Apt and METH (30 min) were determined, as described in Section S3 and Figs. S4–5. The calibration curve for the electrochemical detection of METH on the optimized aptasensor was measured using SWV. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the peak current gradually decreased with the increase of METH concentrations, and the ΔJ were linearly related to the logarithm of METH concentration from 10−3 to 103 ng L−1 with a regression equation of ΔJ = 1.0391 log C + 4.52 and a coefficient of determination R 2 of 0.997 (Fig. 4b). The ΔJ kept constant with increasing METH concentration to 104 ng L−1 and 5 × 104 ng L−1, indicating that the aptasensor reached saturation at 103 ng L−1 (Fig. 4b). The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated to be 0.87 and 2.9 pg L−1, respectively, (LOD = 3σ/k, LOQ = 10σ/k, σ: the standard deviation of the blank signal, and k: the slope of the fitted curve). Compared with the previously reported electrochemical aptasensors (Table S2), the fabricated Au@CDs/CS based aptasensor in this study showed a wider detection range and lower LOD, exhibiting excellent electrochemical performance with high sensitivity for METH detection.

Reproducibility and stability are important parameters to evaluate the device performance for further application. The reproducibility of the aptasensors was investigated by preparing five parallel Apt/Au@CDs/CS/AuE electrodes to analyze 0.1 ng L−1 METH. As displayed in Fig. 4c, the ΔJ of each electrode showed slight changes with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.8 %, indicating good reproducibility of the prepared aptasensors. To evaluate the stability of the prepared aptasensors, the obtained aptasensors were stored at 4 °C and incubated with 0.1 ng L−1 METH for 1, 3, 5, 7, 10, and 15 days, respectively (Fig. 4d). The ΔJ value remained at 94.44 % of the initial strength at the 15th day, confirming that the prepared aptasensors had satisfactory stability. Moreover, the repeatability of the aptasensor was assessed by performing five consecutive measurements of 0.1 ng L−1 METH. The ΔJ with RSD of 3.6 % (Fig. S6a) demonstrated the excellent repeatability of the designed aptasensor. In addition, the regeneration efficiency of the aptasensor was evaluated. The electrodes were regenerated by incubating them in a high-concentration NaCl solution, followed by thorough rinsing with PBS [20]. After each regeneration cycle, the SWV response of the aptasensor was recorded. Although the signal of the modified electrode slightly weakened post-regeneration, it still exhibited a favorable electrochemical response to METH. The RSD across five measurements was 2.9 % (Fig. S6b), further confirming the aptasensor's reliability.

To assess the specificity and selectivity of the aptasensors, HA, SA and BSA as three typical model DOM in aqueous environment were used as coexisting organic interferences of water matrix. As shown in Fig. 4e, when the samples of HA, SA, BSA and METH were incubated on the aptasensors individually, the ΔJ of the target METH (0.1 ng L−1) was significantly higher than that of HA, SA and BSA at 100 mg L−1. Specifically, the ΔJ for METH was 9.8, 170 and 4.3 times greater than that for HA, SA and BSA, respectively, and the concentrations of HA, SA and BSA were 109 times higher than that of METH, which suggested that the constructed aptasensors could specifically recognize METH without the interference of the typical DOMs. In addition, the values of DOC were 44.9 and 41.7 mg L−1 for 100 mg L−1 HA and BSA, which is greatly higher than those of the common real water including the untreated wastewater. It indicated that the constructed aptasensors had satisfactory specificity for METH coexisting with typical DOMs.

The selectivity of the proposed biosensor was studied by introducing HA, SA or BSA in solution with METH of 0.1, 1 and 10 ng L−1. The ΔJ was slightly lower for HA-METH and SA-METH relative to METH alone with an average deviation less than 5 % (Fig. 4f), indicating a negligible effect of humus-like and polysaccharide-like substances on METH detection. In the case of BSA-METH, an increase of ∼13 % of ΔJ was observed compared with that for METH. This could be attributed to the fact that BSA as a protein macromolecule was prone to accumulate on the electrode surface, leading to fouling and obstructing electron transfer [42]. Nevertheless, there were no significant differences for the detection results at METH concentrations of 0.1, 1 and 10 ng L−1 in HA-METH, SA-METH, BSA-METH and METH systems (p = 0.79), when one-way analysis of variance was conducted. Moreover, the DOC concentration of the BSA solution (41.7 mg L−1) was greatly higher than real water samples. It indicated that the detection of METH in aqueous environment by the aptasensor scarcely interfered by the three typical DOMs of water matrix.

Besides, the potential coexistent species in water samples, such as amphetamine (AMP), 3,4-methylenedioxymethamphetamine (MDMA), 3,4-methylenedioxyamphetamine (MDA), 6-acetylmorphine (6-AM), ephedrine (EPH) and pseudoephedrine (PSE), with similar structure to METH were selected to further consider the specificity of the developed aptasensors [43–45]. As shown in Fig. 5 , the ΔJ for 0.1 ng L−1 METH was 3.6–147 times greater than that for the six analogues with concentrations 1 μg L−1. When the mixture of 0.1 ng L−1 METH, 1 μg L−1 six analogues and 100 mg L−1 HA, SA and BSA was incubated on the aptasensors, the ΔJ remained comparable to that of METH alone with variance of 6.5 %. These results implied that with coexistence of high concentrations of molecule analogues and typical DOMs in aqueous environment, the developed aptasensor maintained excellent selectivity due to the highly specific recognition of the aptamer to METH.

Nonetheless, the anti-fouling performance of electrochemical biosensors needed to be further improved to ensure the accuracy of the quantitative results in aqueous environment. The hydrophilicity of electrode surface was associated with a resistance to protein adsorption [42]. The prepared electrodes exhibited weak hydrophilicity as indicated by the water contact angles of 51.16–74.39° (Fig. S7). The effective approaches for pollution resistance could involve tailoring surface morphology and surface chemistry of the electrode using antifouling materials, e.g., polyethylene glycol [46], peptide [47], zwitterionic polymers [48], etc.

Water pH is a crucial factor altering the physio-chemical properties of aptasensor surface and substances in water, and may further affecting the electrochemical response during target detection. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the ΔJ were generally increased with increasing pH from 5 to 8. The pH variation could cause the change of the Apt conformation and the number of binding sites, consequently affecting the Apt-target binding [49,50]. The Apt are generally selected under neutral or physiological pH. At lower pH, depurination or protonation of the binding sites could occur on the Apt, resulting in a partial loss of binding capability of Apt [49]. In addition, the METH with pKa 9.87 tended to be protonated under acidic conditions, which may also result in less frequent of the formation of hydrogen bonds between Apt and METH. Consequently, the dissociation constant of Apt and targets increased and the Apt/target binding was weakened at lower pH [51], leading to the lower ΔJ at acidic pH than neutral and alkaline conditions.

The conformational change of Apt could be investigated by the circular dichroism (CD) spectrum. Fig. 7 a shows CD spectrum of Apt and Apt-METH complex at pH 5 and 8. It can be seen that the Apt displayed typical B-form conformational properties with a negative peak at around 245 nm resulting from the helix force and a positive long wavelength band at 260–290 nm representing the characteristic peak of base stacking [52,53]. The position and amplitudes of the CD bands differed significantly with different conformational properties. The CD bands amplitudes at 245 nm and 260–290 nm decreased clearly at pH 5 relative to initial Apt, which verified the change of the binding conformation of Apt. Although the peak intensity of Apt-METH complex was stronger than Apt alone at pH 5, it is distinctly lower than those at pH 8, which also manifested the Apt-METH binding was more favorable at pH 8 than pH 5.

Additionally, chitosan, the binder in Au@CDs nanocomposites, was stable under neutral and weakly alkaline conditions [54]. On the contrary, when the detection environment was weakly acidic, some chitosan on the electrode surface dissolved, causing a partial loss of the Apt from the electrode surface. This also resulted in a more significant electrochemical response in waters at pH 7–8 than at pH 5–6. These results indicated that the neutral and mildly basic conditions of water samples were in favor of METH determination by the prepared aptasensor. The pH of the real water samples is commonly in the range of 7–8. Therefore, the prepared aptasensor is suitable for METH detection in the real water samples.

The temperature as another important water quality indicator was considered for the METH detection by the aptasensor. The variation of electrochemical signals from 4 to 30 °C is displayed in Fig. 6b. It was generally believed that the temperature could affect the affinity of Apt with targets and the conformation change of the Apt during the binding of Apt and targets. The Apt presented a much higher affinity at lower temperature according to the lower dissociation constants [52,55,56]. Instead, the higher temperature could promote the conformation change of the Apt due to the higher molecular motion energy, which was in favor of the combination of Apt and target [56]. Consequently, the temperature of water samples generally had little effect on METH detection by the developed aptasensor.

The SPM in water may adsorb on the electrode surface of the aptasensor influencing the target test in environment samples [57]. As seen in Fig. 6c, the ΔJ significantly increased with the addition of suspended particles, presenting an escalating trend from 30 to 400 mg L−1 for 0.1 and 1 ng L−1 METH and tending to be stable at 50-400 mg L−1 for 10 ng L−1 METH. It indicated that the suspended particles in waters could significantly interfere with the detection of METH by the aptasensors. The adsorbed suspended particles on the electrode surface could hinder the electron transport and result in the reduction of the current density. When the concentration of the SPM increased to a certain concentration, the SPM adsorption on the surface of the electrode saturated, and consequently the effect of SPM on the electrochemical response achieved equilibrium.

To verify the above hypothesis, the electrodes were incubated with samples containing 400 and 4000 mg L−1 SPM and SPM-METH (0.1 ng L−1), respectively. As shown in Fig. S8, the ΔJ clearly increased with 400 and 4000 mg L−1 SPM alone and the ΔJ of the SPM-METH were significantly higher than METH alone. Moreover, the particles could be seen apparently on the electrode surface from the SEM and atomic force microscope images before and after SPM incubation (Fig. 8 ). These results confirmed that the adherence of the suspended matters on the electrode surface occurred. Compared with METH alone, the ΔJ with SPM-METH elevated substantially. However, there were little difference for the ΔJ between 400 and 4000 mg L−1 SPM (Fig. S8). It confirmed the above conjecture that SPM could adsorb on the electrode surface, influence the METH test, and this effect achieved equilibrium as the SPM adsorption on the electrode surface saturated. To avoid the interference of the SPM in water samples, the precipitation and filtration of water samples or the antifouling treatment are necessary before detecting targets by the aptasensors.

The IS, as an intrinsic parameter of water quality, has been a central theme in many studies for its interface effects in aquatic environments. Fig. 6d showed that the ΔJ changed little with IS below 10 mM, and increased obviously from 10 to 100 mM for 0.1 ng L−1 METH. The METH detection was almost unaffected by the IS below 10 mM at 0.1 ng L−1 METH. However, ΔJ increased clearly with IS increasing for 1 and 10 ng L−1 METH. The aptamer can bind to targets through hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, hydrophobic effects, π-π stacking, van der Waals forces, etc. [58]. The rise in IS reduced the electrostatic repulsion in water, allowing organic molecules to approach the sensing interface more closely through the other attractive forces [59,60], and subsequently led to the increase of ΔJ. Previous research also reported similar phenomenon that the sedimentation rate of Microcystis aeruginosa in porous media increased with heightening the IS in the aquatic environment, due to the suppressed electrostatic interactions between the algae and the porous media [61].

Additionally, higher IS could change the secondary structures of the Apt. Seen in Fig. 7b, the peak intensity of Apt at 240 nm and 260–290 nm markedly reduced at IS 100 mM relative to the original Apt. After binding with METH, the peak intensity of Apt at 260–290 nm increased at IS 100 mM, illustrating the binding of METH and Apt by embedding into the hydrogen bond pocket formed by vertical base pairs [52]. At higher IS, the relative end-to-end distance of both free Apt and bound Apt reduced and the Apt folded into a tightly structure, such as a wound random coil or a hairpin like structure, due to increased electrostatic screening [62]. The conformational change of Apt at higher IS could increase the steric hindrance of electron transfer on the electrode surface, giving rise to the increase of the ΔJ. For real water samples, the IS generally ranges from 0.5 to 5 mM in surface water and about 30 mM in wastewater [63]. The changes of ΔJ were about 5 % with IS below 5 mM for 1 and 10 ng L−1 METH. Thus, the developed electrochemical aptasensor was applicable for the METH test in surface water free from the interference of the IS. The IS in wastewater may cause interference during the target detection.

Four real water samples: drinking water, river water, lake water and untreated wastewater were collected and spiked with three different concentrations of METH (0.1, 1 and 10 ng L−1). As displayed in Table 1 , the recoveries of the METH in tap water and surface water were 85.5–107.0 %, with the average recoveries of 97.3 % in tap water, 102.3 % in river water and 104.6 % in lake water with RSD of 1.2–4.0 %, which were acceptable as the interference of the water matrices in tap water, river water and lake water for trace METH detection was ignorable. The fabricated aptasensor exhibited great adaptability for trace METH monitoring in drinking waters and surface waters.

Although the average recovery (92.4 %) was satisfactory in the wastewater, it was noted that the RSD (5.2–6.2 %) in wastewater were relatively higher than in other water samples. This could be related to the complicated water matrix of the untreated wastewater. From the water quality indicators of the four water samples (Table S3), the pH ranging from 7.27 to 7.90 was suitable for METH detection by the developed aptasensor. Great differences were observed for the turbidity of the four water samples, 175.6 NTU in the wastewater sample and undetected in the other samples. As reported, some illicit drugs had higher proportion on SPM as opposed to in aqueous phase [57]. However, based on the above analysis about the effect of SPM on METH detection, the SPM could adsorb on the surface of the electrode, increasing the test result of the METH. The DOC concentrations were lower for the tap water (1.74 mg L−1) and the river water (5.36 mg L−1), and significantly higher for the lake water (26.9 mg L−1) and the untreated wastewater (11.7 mg L−1). The higher DOC concentration of the lake water may cause slightly higher test results of METH concentration than other water samples.

The excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectra (EEMs) of the four different water samples shown in Fig. S9 stated that the fluorescent peaks of the untreated wastewater at the excitation/emission wavelengths (Ex/Em) of 250/330 nm and 280/335 nm were mainly soluble microbial by-product-like substances with the fluorescence intensities (FI) of about 80000 AU, while the other three water samples mainly contained aromatic protein with highest FI of 5931 AU in tap water, 6011 AU in river water and 9289 AU in lake water at Ex/Em 205/285 nm. Compared with the other three water samples, the untreated wastewater contained extremely large amount of soluble microbial by-product-like substances, which may exert effect on the METH detection by the aptasensor. The composition of the real water is very complicated. To improve the quantitative accuracy of the trace targets in aqueous environment, the water matrix influence and anti-interference capacity as the two vital factors should be considered in the following development of biosensors.

In summary, the sensing platform constructed by introducing the Au@CDs/CS nanocomposite and aptamers on AuE surface for METH detection, possesses many advantages such as wide detection range (10−3–103 ng L−1), low detection limit (0.87 pg L−1), high stability and specificity and good reproducibility. The proposed aptasensor is appropriate for METH determination in aqueous environment with conventional pH 7–8 and stable at wider temperature (4–30 °C) and IS (0–5 mM) ranges, scarcely interfered by the humic acid and polysaccharides-like and protein-like DOM substances of water matrix, while may be affected by suspended solids. Nevertheless, the outstanding properties of the developed aptasensor enable it to detect trace METH in four different water environments (tap water, river water, lake water and wastewater), with the average recoveries from 92.4 % to 104.6 %. This study inspires that the water quality indicators as the crucial influencing elements should be considered in the design and application of the electrochemical biosensors for the determination of trace pollutants in aqueous environment. The anti-interference and anti-fouling performances should be taken seriously when the electrochemical biosensors are further developed for real-time applications in environmental water bodies.

Chunying Yuan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Mushui Shu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Caini Yang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Ding Ding: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Zhizhen Xu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Jingyi Wang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Dongsheng Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
